content.text: App connectors

give a Cowork task goal live, read/write access to the tools your team already uses. Each connector authenticates independently via OAuth and exposes a scoped set of capabilities — reading messages, searching files, drafting emails — that the goal can call as part of its reasoning.
Cowork ships connectors for
Google Drive
Slack
, and
Gmail
in its initial release. These cover the three surfaces most knowledge workers spend the majority of their day: document storage and collaboration, team communication, and email.

This topic walks through each connector in depth: what operations it exposes, how to reference it in a goal, patterns that work well, and limits you need to know before designing a workflow around it.
Google Drive Connector

The Drive connector lets a goal read Google Docs, Sheets, and Slides; traverse folder hierarchies; search by name or content; and write new files or append to existing ones. It authorizes against your personal Google account and respects the same sharing permissions you see in Drive.
What the connector exposes
List and navigate folder contents by path or folder ID
Read the full text of a Google Doc or the cell values of a Sheet
Search for files by name, MIME type, or full-text content query
Create a new Google Doc or Sheet and write content to it
Append rows to an existing Sheet (useful for recurring report targets)
Download a file as plain text or CSV for further analysis
Goal example: synthesise documents from a folder

Task goal:
Read every document in the "Q3 Design Reviews" folder in Google Drive.
For each document, extract: the author (from the file metadata), the
main decision recorded, and any open questions listed.
Compile the results into a new Google Sheet called "Q3 Review Summary"
with columns: Document, Author, Decision, Open Questions.
Share the sheet with design-lead@company.com (view access).

The goal does not need to list file IDs. The engine uses the connector to search Drive, resolves the folder by name, reads each matching Doc in sequence, and creates the output Sheet in one unbroken pass.
Drive connector limits
Reads documents you have permission to access — files shared only inside a workspace domain may require a workspace connector instead
Writing is limited to creating or appending; it cannot overwrite arbitrary blocks inside an existing Doc without reading the whole document first
Binary files (PDFs, images, videos) are not read as text; only Google-native formats and exported-text formats work
Very large Sheets (100k+ rows) may be sampled rather than fully read — design goals to work with named ranges or filtered views
Slack Connector

The Slack connector authorises against a Slack workspace via OAuth. Once connected, a goal can read channel histories, search across the workspace, post messages into channels or threads, and send direct messages.
What the connector exposes
Read recent messages from a public or private channel you are a member of
Search workspace history by keyword, author, date range, or channel
Post a message to a channel (as yourself, not as a bot)
Reply in a thread to an existing message
Read and send direct messages to specific users
List channel members and user profile information
Goal example: weekly channel digest

Task goal:
Read the last 7 days of messages in #product-eng and #design.
Identify decisions made, open questions raised, and action items assigned.
Write a structured digest with three sections — Decisions, Questions, Actions —
and post it as a new message in #weekly-digest.
Also write the same digest to a file called "week-N-digest.md" in the
shared project folder in Google Drive.
This goal uses two connectors: Slack for reading and posting, Drive for writing the archive copy. The engine handles the ordering — read first, synthesise, then write both outputs.
Posting guidelines and limits
Messages are posted under your Slack identity, not a bot account — recipients see your name and can reply to you directly
Channel access is limited to channels you have joined in Slack; the connector does not grant new channel memberships
Message history is typically available for the retention period configured by your workspace admin
Slack's API rate limits apply — goals reading very active channels (10k+ messages/day) may be throttled; design goals around bounded date ranges
File uploads (attaching a PDF or image to a Slack message) are not supported via the connector in the current release
Gmail Connector

The Gmail connector authorises against your Google account and provides access to your inbox, sent mail, drafts, and labels. Goals can search threads, read full message bodies, draft replies, send new emails, and apply labels.
What the connector exposes
Search Gmail using standard search operators (from:, to:, subject:, label:, after:, before:, has:attachment)
Read full thread contents including all replies and quoted text
Draft a new email or reply — saved to Drafts for review before sending
Send an email directly (requires explicit goal instruction to send, not just draft)
Apply or remove Gmail labels on messages or threads
Archive or mark threads as read
Goal example: inbox triage and categorisation

Task goal:
Search my Gmail inbox for unread messages received in the last 3 days.
For each thread, classify it as one of: Action Required, FYI, Vendor, Automated.
For threads classified as "Action Required", draft a brief reply acknowledging
receipt and promising a response within 2 business days.
Apply the appropriate label to each thread and archive anything classified
as Automated.
Drafts are created but not sent unless the goal explicitly includes a send instruction. This gives you a review step before anything leaves your inbox.
Gmail connector limits and safety notes
Sending email is an irreversible action — review goal instructions carefully before enabling direct send
Attachments are accessible as metadata (filename, type, size) but not as readable content; goals cannot process PDF attachments via Gmail
OAuth scope covers only your personal Gmail; Google Workspace shared inboxes require a separate connector configuration
Goals that draft many emails in one run may be slower due to Gmail API per-minute quotas
The connector does not expose calendar invites embedded in email — use the Calendar connector for that
Cross-Connector Workflows

The real power of app connectors emerges when a single goal spans multiple services. The engine sequences connector calls as part of its reasoning — you describe the end result, not the API call order.
Three-connector example: meeting follow-up pipeline

Task goal:
1. Search Gmail for the thread with subject "Project Atlas kickoff" and extract
   the list of attendees and action items from the meeting notes attached.
2. Post a summary of action items to #project-atlas in Slack, tagging each owner.
3. Create a Google Sheet called "Atlas Action Items" with columns:
   Item, Owner, Due Date, Status — pre-populate Status as "Open".
4. Send each owner an individual Gmail with their specific action items listed.

Connectors needed: Gmail (read), Slack (post), Drive (write), Gmail (send).

Notice that Gmail appears twice — once for reading and once for sending. The engine resolves this as two separate connector operations within the same goal. You do not need to break this into multiple goals.
Sequencing and dependencies

When a goal uses multiple connectors, the engine infers the dependency order from the goal text. If the Drive write depends on reading Slack first, write the goal in that order — the engine follows narrative sequence. If two connector calls are independent (post to Slack AND write to Drive), the engine may parallelise them.
State the primary data source first, the transformations in the middle, and the outputs last
Explicitly name connector operations that must happen in sequence ("then post", "after writing")
Avoid ambiguous pronoun references across connector steps ("read it" after mentioning both a Doc and a thread — be explicit)
Authorising Connectors

Each connector is authorized once through the Cowork settings panel and remains active for all future goals in your workspace. Authorization is per-user and per-workspace — a connector enabled for you is not shared with teammates unless they individually authorize their own accounts.
Authorization flow
Open Settings → Connectors in Cowork
Select the connector (Drive, Slack, or Gmail) and click Connect
Complete the OAuth flow in your browser — grant only the scopes listed
The connector appears as Active in the settings panel
Reference it in any goal immediately — no restart needed
Scope and access model
Connector
OAuth Account
Access Scope
Shared with teammates?
Google Drive
Google account (personal or Workspace)
Files and folders you can access in Drive
No — each teammate connects their own account
Slack
Slack workspace account
Channels you have joined; DMs you can access
No — per-user authorization
Gmail
Google account (personal or Workspace)
Your personal inbox and sent/draft/labels
No — per-user authorization

If you need a goal to access shared resources (a team Drive folder, a shared inbox), ensure the authorizing account has the relevant permissions in the underlying service — Cowork cannot elevate permissions beyond what OAuth grants.
Common Usage Patterns
Pattern
Connectors Used
Typical Cadence
Example Goal
Inbox triage
Gmail
Daily
Classify unread threads, draft replies for Action Required
Channel digest
Slack, Drive
Weekly
Summarise #channels into Drive doc
Document research
Drive
On demand
Read folder of docs, extract structured data
Meeting prep
Gmail, Drive
Before each meeting
Find email thread + briefing doc, produce agenda
Status broadcast
Drive, Slack
Weekly
Read status sheet, post summary to #team-updates
Action item tracking
Gmail, Slack, Drive
After each meeting
Extract items, post to Slack, create tracker Sheet
Handling Connector Errors in Goals
Connectors can fail for several reasons: expired OAuth tokens, revoked permissions, API rate limits, or network issues. Goals can include fallback instructions to handle these gracefully.

Task goal:
Read the "Engineering Weekly" Google Sheet and extract rows where Status = "Blocked".
If the Drive connector is unavailable, check the #eng-status Slack channel for the
most recent "status update" message and use that instead.
Write the list of blocked items to a new file called "blocked-items.md" in the
shared workspace folder.

Fallback: if both Drive and Slack are unavailable, write a placeholder file
noting the date and that data was not retrieved, so the output history stays consistent.
This pattern — primary source, fallback source, fallback output — keeps recurring goals from silently skipping runs when a connector is temporarily unavailable.
Expired OAuth: re-authorize in Settings → Connectors; existing goals resume on next run
Rate limit hit mid-goal: the engine pauses and retries automatically for transient limits
Permission revoked: the engine reports the specific file or channel it could not access
Workspace connector disabled: contact your Cowork workspace admin to re-enable
Summary

Google Drive, Slack, and Gmail are the three app connectors available in Cowork. Each authorises independently via OAuth and exposes a specific set of read and write operations. Goals can reference multiple connectors in a single instruction, and the engine sequences the necessary API calls as part of its reasoning.
Drive: read and write Docs and Sheets, traverse folders, search by name or content
Slack: read channel history, post messages and thread replies, search the workspace
Gmail: search and read threads, draft and send email, apply labels
Cross-connector goals work by describing the full workflow in natural language; the engine infers call order
Each connector authorises per-user via OAuth — access is bounded by the permissions of the authorising account
